summary: DHH argues that modern culture has developed an accelerating addiction to 'The Current Villain' — a rotating scapegoat who absorbs collective outrage on social media. Drawing on René Girard's mimetic theory and scapegoating mechanism, he explains how figures like Chapelle, Kanye West, and Elon Musk serve as sacrificial targets to discharge group tension over unresolved cultural issues. He contends that these scapegoating cycles are accelerating because they fail to address underlying conflicts, requiring ever-more-frequent targets. He ends with dark humor, suggesting either the destruction of Twitter or the second coming of Christ as potential remedies.
key_points: Culture has shifted from shared moments of glory to shared villains, with 'The Current Villain' consuming more of collective attention than broader cultural topics
René Girard's scapegoating mechanism explains how communities project unresolved tensions onto individuals who violate group orthodoxy, regardless of actual guilt
The cycle is accelerating because targeting major public figures who can fight back provides less tension relief than early cancel culture's focus on minor characters
The shift from music and culture as shared centers to political outrage as the binding force reflects a fragmented society desperate for common ground
The scapegoating mechanism is self-perpetuating: unresolved underlying issues guarantee the need for new scapegoats with increasing frequency
money_quotes: But humans still need something to come together around, thus: The Current Thing.
Worse than the broad category of The Current Thing is the subcategory of The Current Villain.
This singular focus on an individual that has to absorb all our hate, our rage, our frustration with reality as we've come to see it.

The scapegoats barely release any tension any more, especially since the shift from the early day of cancel culture that focused on minor characters with limited power to resist the mob, to major characters who can actually fight back.
Mobs form with ever greater frequency and ferocity in ever-more desperate attempts to dissolve the tension and restore unification. But it's just not working.
So I guess all we need to resolve the situation is the second coming of Christ? Let me say a little prayer.
key_insight: Modern social media scapegoating cycles are accelerating because targeting prominent figures fails to resolve the underlying cultural tensions driving collective outrage, creating an insatiable demand for new villains.
